coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots as sulfiphilic and lithiophilic mediator toward high stability and durable life lithium-sulfur batteries
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by ELSEVIER B.V. and Science Press. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by ELSEVIER B.V. and Science Press.
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Chaojiang
Yang, Rong
Huang, Yong
Mao, Lei
Yang, Yuanyuan
Gong, Le
Dong, Xin
Yan, Yinglin
Zou, Yiming
Zhong, Lisheng
Xu, Yunhua
coredata.dc:description: The development of lithium-sulfur (Li-S) battery as one of the most attractive energy storage systems among lithium metal batteries is seriously hindered by low sulfur utilization, poor cycle stability and uneven redeposition of Li anode. It is necessary to propose strategies to address the problems as well as improve the electrochemical performance. One of the effective solutions is to improve the sulfiphilicity of sulfur cathode and the lithiophilicity of the Li anode. Herein, we reported that a synergistic functional separator (graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-polyacrylonitrile (PAN) @polypropylene (PP) separator) improved the electrochemical activity of sulfur cathode as well as the stability of Li anode. GQDs induced uniform Li+ nucleation and deposition, which slowed down the passivation of Li anode and avoided short-circuit. Further, three-dimensional network constructed by electrospinning nanofibers and the polar functional groups of GQDs could both effectively inhibit the shuttle of LiPSs and improve the sulfur utilization. The stability of Li-S battery was improved by the synergistic effect. In addition, GQDs and electrospinning nanofibers protector increased lifetime of separators. Benefiting from the unique design strategy, Li//Li symmetric battery with GQDs-PAN@PP separators exhibited stably cycling for over 600 h. More importantly, the Li-S full batteries based GQDs-PAN@PP separators enabled high stability and desirable sulfur electrochemistry, including high reversibility of 558.09 mA h g−1 for 200 cycles and durable life with a low fading rate of 0.075% per cycle after 500 cycles at 0.5 C. Moreover, an impressive areal capacity of 3.23 mA h cm−2 was maintained under high sulfur loading of 5.10 mg cm−2. This work provides a new insight for modification separator to improve the electrochemical performance of Li-S/Li metal batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-sulfur batteries
Separator
Graphene quantum dots
Cycle stability
Durable life

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology structure of GQDs and separators
Possible mechanism of sulfiphilic action of GQDs
Mechanism of lithium affinity of GQDs-PAN@PP separators

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The DFT calculations were supported by Prof. Wei Wang at XAUT and Dr. Jingshuang Dang at SNNU. This work was supported by Key Program (U20A20235) funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52171127, 51974242), Regional Innovation Capability Guidance Program of Shaanxi Provincial Government (2022QFY10-06), Key R&D Program of Xianyang Science and Technology Bureau (2021ZDYF-GY-0029), and Doctoral dissertation innovation fund (310-252072201) by Xi'an University of Technology.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-vis absorption (black), PLE, PL spectra of GQDs solutions, the inset is the optical photograph of GQDs with 365 nm UV lamp. (b) XRD pattern, (c) FTIR spectra, (d) XPS survey spectrum, (e–g) TEM images, and (h) SAED pattern of GQDs. (i) Synthesis schematic illustration of GQDs-PAN@PP separator and its advantages as a dual-function separator for Li-S batteries.

(a) XRD pattern of PP, PAN@PP, and GQDs-PAN@PP. Comparative histograms of (b) porosity and (c) electrolyte uptake for various separators. (d) Dynamic contact angle measurement. (e–j) SEM images of different separators. (k–m) TEM images of GQDs-PAN.

(a) Rate capabilities, the corresponding (b) GCD curves (GQDs-PAN@PP separators), and (c) polarization potential differences of the cells with the PP, PAN@PP, and GQDs-PAN@PP separators at different current densities. (d) Comparison of initial GCD curves at 0.1 C for cells with different separators. (e) The details of charge/discharge curves at 0.1 C. (f) The value and ratio of Q L/Q H calculated from the GCD curves of the cells with various separators. (g) The cycling stability of cells with different separators at 0.1 C. (h) Cycling performance under 0.1 C of GQDs-PAN@PP separators with high sulfur loading. (i) Long-term cycling performance of the cells at 0.5 C. (j) Performance comparison between GQDs-PAN@PP separators with other published work.

(a) CV curves of Li-S batteries with various separators at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1, (b) the corresponding voltage of redox peaks, and the enlarged view of CV curves in (c) peak II and (d) peak III. Tafel plots calculated from (e) reduction peak II and (f) oxidation peak III. (g) CV plots at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. (h) Nyquist plots of cells based with different separators and (i) the corresponding linear relationship between Z′ and ω −0.5.

(a) Schematic of sulfiphilic and lithiophilic action mechanism of GQDs-PAN@PP membranes as Li-S batteries separators. Microtopography analysis of the cells with PP, PAN@PP, and GQDs-PAN@PP separators after 200 cycles: (b–d) sulfur cathode, (e–g) separators, (h–j) lithium anode.

The visualization polysulfide permeation tests for (a) PP, (b) PAN@PP, and (c) GQDs-PAN@PP separators with double-L device. (d) XPS survey, (e) C 1s, and (f) S 2p spectra of GQDs-PAN@PP separators after 200 cycles. (g) The binding energies between Li2S x and S8 to GQDs, PAN, and GQDs-PAN calculated from DFT. (h) The molecular models of the interaction between GQDs-PAN and Li2S via the first-principles. (i) The energy density distribution of GQDs-PAN after absorbing.

Li stripping and plating behavior of Li//Li symmetric cells based on PP, PAN@PP, and GQDs-PAN@PP separators. (a) Cycle stability of the Li//Li symmetric cells for varied separators at 0.5 mA cm−2 and stationary capacity of 0.5 mA h cm−2. (b) Rate capability of Li//Li symmetric cells of GQDs-PAN@PP separator at step-increased current densities from 0.5 to 4 mA cm−2. (c) Corresponding enlarged voltage plots at different current densities. (d) Schematic illustration of GQDs-PAN@PP separators effectiveness protection to Li-metal.

SEM images of the cycled Li anode (The SEM images were tested on the side without contact with the GQDs-PAN modification layer) in Li//Li symmetric cells (a–c) and the deposited Li on Cu foil (d–f) after 200 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 (1 mA h cm−2) in Li//Cu cells. (g) Li ions transference number tests of GQDs-PAN@PP separators. (h) Voltage profiles (inset: amplified) and (i) coulombic efficiency of Li//Cu cells equipped with PP, PAN@PP, and GQDs-PAN@PP separators in the lithium plating and stripping process during the initial cycle at 1 mA cm−2 with a constant capacity of 1 mA h cm−2.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots as sulfiphilic and lithiophilic mediator toward high stability and durable life lithium-sulfur batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Research Center for Composite and Intelligent Manufacturing Technology, Institute of Chemical Power Sources, Xi'an University of Technology, Xi'an 710048, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: International Research Center for Composite and Intelligent Manufacturing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: International Research Center for Composite and Intelligent Manufacturing Technology, Institute of Chemical Power Sources, Xi'an University of Technology, Xi'an 710048, Shaanxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi'an University of Technology, Xi'an 710048, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Xi'an University of Technology, Xi'an 710048, Shaanxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Yulin University, Yulin 719000, Shaanxi, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy Engineering, Yulin University, Yulin 719000, Shaanxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The designed GQDs-PAN@PP separator can synergistically improve the electrochemical activity of sulfur cathode and regulate stripping/deposition behavior of Li anode which endows the LSBs ultra-low decay rate of 0.075% after 500 cycles at 0.5 C, stable cycling performance for 600 h, and impressive areal capacity with high sulfur loading.

The development of lithium-sulfur (Li-S) battery as one of the most attractive energy storage systems among lithium metal batteries is seriously hindered by low sulfur utilization, poor cycle stability and uneven redeposition of Li anode. It is necessary to propose strategies to address the problems as well as improve the electrochemical performance. One of the effective solutions is to improve the sulfiphilicity of sulfur cathode and the lithiophilicity of the Li anode. Herein, we reported that a synergistic functional separator (graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-polyacrylonitrile (PAN) @polypropylene (PP) separator) improved the electrochemical activity of sulfur cathode as well as the stability of Li anode. GQDs induced uniform Li+ nucleation and deposition, which slowed down the passivation of Li anode and avoided short-circuit. Further, three-dimensional network constructed by electrospinning nanofibers and the polar functional groups of GQDs could both effectively inhibit the shuttle of LiPSs and improve the sulfur utilization. The stability of Li-S battery was improved by the synergistic effect. In addition, GQDs and electrospinning nanofibers protector increased lifetime of separators. Benefiting from the unique design strategy, Li//Li symmetric battery with GQDs-PAN@PP separators exhibited stably cycling for over 600 h. More importantly, the Li-S full batteries based GQDs-PAN@PP separators enabled high stability and desirable sulfur electrochemistry, including high reversibility of 558.09 mA h g−1 for 200 cycles and durable life with a low fading rate of 0.075% per cycle after 500 cycles at 0.5 C. Moreover, an impressive areal capacity of 3.23 mA h cm−2 was maintained under high sulfur loading of 5.10 mg cm−2. This work provides a new insight for modification separator to improve the electrochemical performance of Li-S/Li metal batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: As an ideal anode for high energy density batteries, lithium metal has low electrochemical potential (−3.04 V vs. NHE), low gravimetric density (0.534 g cm−3) and ultrahigh theoretical capacity (3860 mA h g−1 or 2061 mA h cm−3) [1,2]. These extraordinary advantages lead lithium metal to be one of the best anode candidates for energy storage devices [3]. In fact, the Li anode is seriously affected by the uneven Li+ flux and solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) in practice [4]. The inhomogeneous Li+ deposition during the process of electrochemical reaction induces untoward effects, such as the growth of lithium dendrites, deposition of “dead Li”, and electrolyte consumption, which will cause the capacity attenuation, polarization intensified and even internal short circuit [3,5,6].

Among lithium metal battery energy storage systems, one of the most promising next-generation electrochemical energy storage batteries [7,8], lithium-sulfur (Li-S) battery with elemental sulfur as the cathode has attracted comprehensive attention attributed to its high theoretical specific capacity (1675 mA h g−1) and energy density (2600 W h kg−1). Unfortunately, Li-S batteries also suffer from poor cycling stability and short life due to the “shuttle effect” caused by the dissolution and migration of intermediate lithium polysulfides (LiPSs) and uneven Li+ deposition in the electrochemical reaction process. Meanwhile, the dissolved LiPSs migrated freely to anode through the PP separators and were reduced to low-order soluble polysulfide or insoluble Li2S/Li2S2 covering on the Li anode, resulting in the loss of active sulfur and deterioration of capacity, which further exacerbated the corrosion and passivation of the Li anode [9–11].

To date, several strategies for materials design and structure optimization have been proposed to solve the problems of Li anode (e.g., Li anode structural construction [12–14], artificial SEI layers design [15–17], and electrolyte modification [18–21]) and sulfur cathode (e.g., hosts material design [22–25], functionalized interlayer/separators coating [26–30], and advanced binders [31,32]). Nevertheless, most of these targeted strategies only focus on the problem of Li anode or sulfur cathode. Few research have achieved synergistic improvement of anode and cathode performance in one battery [33,34]. Consequently, it is of great significance to design and prepare multifunctional component with synergistic reinforcement both of Li anode and sulfur cathode.

As one of the indispensable components of battery, separator is the pivot of material transport between Li anode and sulfur cathode, and plays the core role of electrochemical transport while avoiding short circuit. Functionalizing separator is identified as an efficient and convenient strategy for co-regulation of lithium dissolution/deposition and electrochemical reactions of active sulfur [29,30]. It is desirable to construct a functional separator with sulfiphilic/lithiophilic and rational ordered spatial structure to face the both problems of sulfur cathode and Li anode, accelerating sulfur redox kinetics and adjusting the lithium deposition behavior synchronously, and finally improve the electrochemical performance of Li-S battery which is of amazing significance but still remain great challenges.

Among nanomaterials engineering, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with ultra-small size represent one of the new members of the carbon material family [35], which have been proven that it can greatly enhance the interaction between host and guest, reduce the reaction energy barrier [36]. It also has excellent adsorption effects on LiPSs and regulation of the active site at the electrochemical interfaces due to its unique quantum linkage [37,38]. Benefiting from these properties, GQDs have been widely studied and applied in Li-S batteries, Li-ion batteries, and sodium-ion batteries [5,18,39–41], while the exploration of GQDs for simultaneous improvement of sulfur cathode and Li anode electrochemistry has rarely been reported before.

Herein, GQDs was introducing into the functionalized separators to deal with the problems of sulfur cathode and Li anode and to upgrade the stability and the lifetime of Li-S battery. GQDs with quantum confinement effect and polar functional group as lithiophilic mediator induce uniform lithium nucleation and redeposition, which inhibit lithium dendrite and avoid short circuit. Simultaneously, the three-dimensional network constructed by electrospinning nanofibers and the polar functional groups of GQDs as sulfiphilic mediator can effectively adsorb LiPSs, inhibit the “shuttle effect” and prevent the passivation of the anode by polysulfides. With the unique design strategy, the GQDs-PAN@PP multifunctional separator is endowed with high porosity of 77.84%, electrolyte uptake of 228.34%, and ionic conductivity of 1.09 mS cm−1. The Li//Li cells with GQDs-PAN@PP separator exhibit stable cycling over 600 h with minimal voltage hysteresis. More importantly, the Li-S batteries based optimized GQDs-PAN@PP separator enables high stability and desirable sulfur electrochemistry, including high reversibility of 558.09 mA h g−1 for 200 cycles at 0.1 C and durable life with a low fading rate of 0.075% each cycle after 500 cycles at 0.5 C. Moreover, an impressive areal capacity of 3.23 mA h cm−2 is maintained under high sulfur loading of 5.10 mg cm−2 after 50 cycles.

Preparation of GQDs-PAN@PP Separators. The GQDs solution was synthesized by a top-down strategy as our previous report [41]. And GQDs powder was obtained after freeze-drying over 24 h. For the preparation of electrospinning solution, 0.2 g GQDs powder and 2 g polyacrylonitrile (PAN) were dispersed in 20 mL N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) in turn and stirred at 50 °C to form a homogenous solution. The solution was held in plastic syringe, and the solution injection rate was controlled by constant-flow syringe pump. And the commercial polypropylene (PP, Celgard 2320) separator wound on the roller was used to collect the nanofibers. A series of GQDs-PAN@PP separators was obtained by adjusting and controlling the parameters of electrospinning. The PAN@PP separator was obtained with optimized condition. After drying, the separators were pressed with a roller machine and cut into circular separator with diameter of 16 mm. All of electrospinning processes were done under ambient conditions.

Preparation of S/C composites. The composites were prepared by conventional melt and diffusion method [22]. The sulfur and conductive carbon (Super-P) with a mass ratio of 6:4 was ground and mixed evenly and then was placed into the reactor and treated at 155 °C for 12 h to obtain sulfur/carbon (S/C) composites.

Material characterization. The porosity of the GQDs-PAN@PP separators was measured via the uptake experiment with n-butanol [41]. The thickness of separators was measured and recorded with digimatic micrometer. The porosity was calculated by the quality change of the separator before and after soaking in n-butanol solution for 3 h under ambient conditions with PP separator as blank. Three groups of parallel experiments were conducted and the average value was taken. The calculation equation of the porosity (P) as follows.
(1)
P
( % )
=
(
W a
-
W b
)
/
(
ρ b
×
V s
)
× 100 %
where Wa and Wb are the weights of the separator after and before soaking in n-butanol, respectively. ρb is the density of n-butanol solution and Vs is the volume of the GQDs-PAN@PP separator.

Dynamic contact angle measurement and analysis of liquid electrolyte contact angle (CA) for series separators were conducted by optical contact angle measuring instrument (China, Shanghai, JC2000D1). Measurement of liquid electrolyte uptake was carried out by immersing the separators in the electrolyte solution for 1 h. The residual electrolyte retaining on the surface of the separators was removed by softly wiping with weighing paper. The separators were repeatedly weighed until the mass steadily. The ratio of electrolyte uptake (EU) was defined as the weight ratio of the liquid uptake to the dried separator and calculated with Eq. (2).
(2)
EU
%
=
(
W a
-
W b
)
/
W b
× 100 %
where Wa and Wb are the mass of the separators after and before steeping in electrolyte, respectively. The measurement of all samples was based on the average data of five times for date consistency.

The crystalline structure of the samples was investigated by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, XRD-6100). The fluorescence property of GQDs was measured by fluorescence spectrometer (Rf-6000) and ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV-vis, WFH-203). The chemical states of GQDs were procured with Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, FTIR-S900) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS-ULTRA). Membrane thickness was measured with digimatic micrometer (MDC-25MX). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6700F) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was performed to characterize the surface morphologies and corresponding elemental mappings of separators. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-3010) was applied for microstructure of GQDs in detail.

Electrochemical measurement. The electrochemical performance of the separators was evaluated by CR2025 type coin cells. The S/C composites, conductive agent (KS-6) and binder (Polyvinylidene difluoride, PVDF) were added into solvent (N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone, NMP) with mass ratio of 7:2:1 to form homogeneous slurry, then coated on the aluminum current collector. Finally, the S/C electrode was punched into circular discs of 10 mm as cathode with sulfur areal loading of 1.40 mg cm−2 (Table S1). The experimentally prepared samples were applied as separators. The cell was assembled in the glove box with lithium metal foil as anode and 1 M LiTFSI in 1,2-dimethoxyethane and 1,3-dioxolane (1:1, v/v) with 1 wt.% LiNO3 as electrolyte. The contents of oxygen and water in the glove box were both below 0.01 ppm. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were operated with IviumStat electrochemical workstation (IviumStat 20V). The galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) and cycle performance were tested with Neware battery test system (CT-4008T). Additionally, the specific capacity in this work was calculated on the basis of mass of sulfur.

Visualization permeation experiment and theoretical calculation. The visualization permeation experiment was carried out in a double-L device and theoretical calculation further assisted to prove the adsorption effect of GQDs-PAN@PP separators on LiPSs in Li2S6 solution (0.02 M). The experimental phenomena were recorded with digital camera. All operations were performed in the glove box. The density functional theory (DFT) calculation using Materials Studio Dmol3 model revealed the adsorption effect of GQDs-PAN to polysulfides [42]. The adsorption energy (E ads) was computed to measure the adsorption strength between the substrate (GQDs, PAN, and GQDs-PAN) and the LiPSs. It can be expressed as
(3)
E
ads
=
E
total
-
E
sub
-
E
LiPSs
where E total, E sub, and E LiPSs represent the energy of the substrate-LiPSs adsorbed system, the energy of the substrate and the energy of the LiPSs. With this definition, a more negative adsorption energy indicates a more energetically favorable adsorption interaction of the LiPSs with the substrate. The more details see the supporting information.

Experimental design of optimization variables for electrospinning parameters. The effects of electrospinning parameters on porosity and CA of series samples were studied by Box-Behnken design (BBD) of standard response surface methodology (RSM). As illustrated in Table S2, applied voltage, feed rate, and spinning time were selected as variables. The ranges of the variables were selected from preliminary one-factor tests. The experiment plan and analysis of variance were designed and analyzed by Design-Expert 12. The experimental design schemes and response value were shown in Table S3. The experiment was designed for 17 runs, in which the factorial experiment was performed 12 times and the central point experiment was repeated 5 times to evaluate experimental errors. The porosity and CA values obtained from the experiments were the objective response variables (R).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology structure of GQDs and separators
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs solution (Fig. S1) with functional groups was synthesized by top-down strategy, and GQDs powder was obtained after freeze drying. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the UV-vis absorption spectrum curve (black) of GQDs solution has two typical absorption peaks at 228 and 299 nm, corresponding to the π → π* transition of graphite sp 2 and the n → π* transition of polar functional groups, respectively, indicating the characteristics of polar groups with the prepared GQDs [43]. In addition, GQDs solution has obvious fluorescence effect under irradiated with 365 nm UV lamp (inset) and photoluminescence, which is consistent with literature reports [44]. The diffraction peak at 22° of the XRD pattern (Fig. 1b) corresponds to the (002) plane of the graphite, presenting the graphitized structure of the GQDs, which will be beneficial to improve the ion and electron diffusion in the cell. The Raman spectra of GQDs are shown in Fig. S2. The D band is caused by the defect and disorder induction at 1361 cm−1 and the G band is produced by the stretching motion of the sp 2 hybridization at 1590 cm−1, both of which are typical characteristic peaks of the carbon materials [45]. Furthermore, the FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1c) and XPS (Fig. 1d and Fig. S3) characterization further indicated that GQDs contained plentiful oxygenic and nitrogenous functional groups [46]. Abundant functional groups can effectively adsorb polysulfides and improve the cycle stability of the cell. To further investigate the microstructure of GQDs, TEM measurement was performed in detail. It is obvious that GQDs particles in TEM images are well dispersed, with an average particle size about 2.30 nm (Fig. 1e, f and Fig. S4). Moreover, the lattice fringes can be seen in high-resolution TEM (Fig. 1g) with a lattice spacing of about 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (1120) crystal plane of graphene [47]. However, the absence of bright diffraction spots in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern suggests that an amorphous structure of GQDs is due to its abundant polar functional groups. The results are consistent with the XRD pattern showing only wide diffraction peaks.

The effect of electrospinning parameters on the porosity and CA of the separators was investigated by RSM analysis. The variance analysis and significance test for the influence of spinning parameters on porosity and CA are shown in Tables S4 and S5. The normal plot of the residuals between the experimental actual and predicted value of the regression model further indicates that the model accords with the distribution law, and can well describe the influence of various factors on the porosity and CA of the separators (Figs. S5 and S6). In addition, the reliability of the response surface analysis results is proven by analyzing the multi-factor perturbation curve (Fig. S7) and the 3D surface response graph (Fig. S8) of the regression model. Ultimately, the optimized parameters were determined. The GQDs-PAN@PP separators obtained under optimized conditions were applied as the separators for Li-S batteries (Fig. 1i), which can play dual-function synergistic effect (inducing uniform deposition of Li+ and adsorbing LiPSs to inhibit shuttling), thus improving the electrochemical performance of the Li-S batteries.

Compared with commercial PP and PAN@PP separators, the XRD diffraction pattern of GQDs-PAN@PP separator shows the diffraction peak of carbon crystal surface (002) at 25°, further indicating that GQDs was incorporated into PAN nanofibers (Fig. 2 a). The result of RSM proves that the applied voltage, feeding speed, and spinning time affect the physicochemical properties of the separators, such as fiber structure, thermal stability, electrolyte uptake, CA, porosity, and ion transport. Therefore, five groups (Table S6) of samples were selected to be characterized according to the optimization results and experimental conditions, and the influence of electrospinning parameters on the performance of the separator was comparatively explored. The porosity has a significant effect on the electrolyte uptake, the contact between electrolyte and separators, the Li+ transport, and further electrochemical performance. The comparison of the porosity of separators is shown in Fig. 2(b). The porosity of PP separator is 41.24%, while that of the spinning nanofibers increased the porosity of separators. Moreover, with the applied voltage growth from 12 to 18 kV, the porosity of the separator increases from 46.02% (GQDs-PAN1@PP) to 57.65% (GQDs-PAN3@PP), which may be caused by the increase of crosslinking structure of electrospinning nanofibers. It is noteworthy that GQDs-PAN@PP has the highest porosity (77.84%), indicating that the GQDs-PAN spinning nanofibers have abundant pore structure under optimized conditions, which is conducive to electrolyte infiltration. The higher electrolyte uptake of the separator, the more electrolyte can be absorbed and stored, which is conducive to improving the Li+ transmission. Fig. 2(c) displayed the bar contrast chart of electrolyte uptake of the separators. It exhibits that the electrolyte uptake of PP separator is 116.67%, while the electrolyte uptake of the GQDs-PAN@PP separator is 228.34%, which further indicates that the fiber layer formed under optimized condition has a good wettability with the electrolyte. It is beneficial to improve the ionic conductivity and the capacity of cells.

The CA test can reflect the electrolyte wettability of the separators more intuitively. Fig. 2(d) exhibits the digital photo of CA measurement of the separators. In order to keep consistent with the response value of the RSM, the value of CA is represented by the obtuse angle. It is observed that the electrolyte droplets on the PP separator are oval, and there is no significant change after infiltration for 2 s. The CA value of PP separator is small (148.21°), indicating that the PP separator has a large surface tension and poor electrolyte wettability. The CA of PAN@PP and GQDs-PAN@PP separators are larger than that of PP separator, which points out that spinning nanofibers have better electrolyte wettability and can provide more electrolyte storage space. In addition, the spinning voltage increased from 12 to 18 kV while CA decreased to 164.30° (GQDs-PAN3@PP), suggesting that the nanofibers formed at high voltage are not conducive to improving the contact with electrolyte. Interestingly, both PAN@PP and GQDs-PAN@PP separators prepared with the optimized spinning parameters have large CA value, further illustrating the accuracy of the RSM. More importantly, the GQDs-PAN@PP separator with the largest CA (170.14°) is due to the interaction between GQDs and PAN nanofibers to form the uniform fiber skeleton structure possibly.

The digital photograph of separators was shown in Fig. S9. Obviously, PAN and GQDs-PAN nanofibers adhered tightly to the surface of PP separator. In Fig. S10, the thickness of PAN and GQDs-PAN nanofibers modified layer is about 10 μm. The areal loading amount of GQDs-PAN modified layer on the PP separators is 0.58 mg cm−2. Furthermore, the microstructure of the separators was characterized by SEM in detail. It is observed that the PP separator has distinct slit holes on surface (Fig. 2e). It is well-known that the holes of PP separator ensured the transmission of Li+ as well as polysulfide, resulting in shuttling and poor cycle stability of batteries. The slit holes of PP can be modified by electrospinning nanofibers with the microstructure, which adjust accessibility by parameters of electrospinning. As depicted in Fig. 2(h), under the condition of low voltage (12 kV), the diameter of GQDs-PAN1@PP fibers is larger and presents a clustered state, which is motived by the insufficient tensile force of the electric field on the spinning fluid jet. The nanofibers obtained at 15 kV are evenly distributed and crosslinked to each other into a network structure. When the voltage increases to 18 kV, the diameter of the nanofibers decreases. The results show that a certain intensity of voltage leads to the polymer surface with more charge collection, while the mutually exclusive charge brings out the electric field force to stretch the fluid more strongly, which will be conducive to the uniform distribution of the fiber and the construction of the network skeleton [48,49]. The uniform distribution of nanofibers and the interconnected network structure can restrain LiPSs shuttle and promote the absorption and storage of electrolyte, which is consistent with the physical characterization results of the separators. In addition, with the extension of spinning time, the disorder degree of spatial distribution of nanofibers increases. More importantly, the fibers distribution of GQDs-PAN@PP separators (Fig. 2g) is more uniform than that of PAN@PP (Fig. 2f), further demonstrating the important role of GQDs in the crosslinking structure formation of nanofibers. The diameter of nanofibers is about 0.25 μm and the distribution is more uniform, which is consistent with the particle size analysis results via nano measure from SEM images (Fig. S12). Fig. S11 exhibits the SEM image of GQDs-PAN@PP separator with a multiple of 60 K and the corresponding element mapping. In addition, there are abundant N and O elements in element mapping, which is beneficial to the chemisorption of LiPSs. The distribution of GQDs in PAN nanofibers was characterized by TEM. Fig. 2(k–m) show that the nanoscale GQDs are coated and evenly distributed along the nanofibers.

Li-S batteries were assembled with PP, PAN@PP, and GQDs-PAN@PP separators to explore the mechanism of the electrochemical performance improvement, and verify the rationality and effectiveness of design. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 (a), the rate performance of cells based various separators is measured at current densities ranging from 0.1 to 2 C rate (1 C = 1675 mA g−1). The cell with PP separator exhibited low specific discharge capacities of 818.06, 370.08, 211.10, 136.54, and 87.97 mA h g−1 under current rates of 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 C with a low active sulfur utilization of only 5%–48%, respectively. Inactive sulfur and incompletely converted LiPSs can act as electrical resistors in subsequent cycles due to their low conductivity, exacerbating the “shuttling effect” [50]. Nevertheless, there are markedly changes after the electrospinning nanofibers were modified. With the nanofibers as the stable layer, the capacities of the cell with PAN@PP separator increased to 1022.42, 557.18, 426.78, 337.45, and 271.33 mA h g−1 under the same conditions. More importantly, the cell with the GQDs-PAN@PP separator revealed higher capacities of 1286.07, 805.83, 594.20, 496.32, and 375.56 mA h g−1, respectively, at the corresponding current densities, with spectacular sulfur utilization of 22%–77%. When the current rate regained to 0.2 C, a capacity of 597.61 mA h g−1 can be maintained, implying electrochemical reversibility and high stability. The GCD curves of PP, PAN@PP, and GQDs-PAN@PP cells at different current densities are shown in Fig. S13 and Fig. 3(b), at the current densities of 2 C, the typical discharge voltage plateaus can still be represented in GQDs-PAN@PP cell. By comparison, the PP and PAN@PP cells display a significant voltage polarization potential (denoted by ΔE) (Fig. 3c). And as the current density was increased, the polarization became even more evident. Regardless of the current density increasing, the lower polarization of the GQDs-PAN@PP cells testified improved oxidation and reduction kinetics and good ion conductivity [51]. The results can be attributed to the collaborative optimization of GQDs-PAN nanofibers. Fig. 3(d and e) exhibit the typical GCD curves tested at 0.1 C for the batteries with PP, PAN@PP, and GQDs-PAN@PP separators, respectively. The two plateaus in the discharge curve correspond to the transformation of S8 to long chain Li2S x (4 ≤ x ≤ 6) and Li2S x to short chain Li2S/Li2S2 in the electrochemical process, respectively (Fig. S14) [52]. The plateaus in the charge curve correspond to the reversible reactions. It is noteworthy that a distinct valley can be behold between the two discharge platforms, defined as “nucleation point” of Li2S (Fig. 3e and Fig. S15) [53]. The nucleation reaction kinetics can be evaluated based on the difference between the nucleation point and tangential line of the second voltage platform [54]. The cell with GQDs-PAN@PP separator exhibited a lower value (29 mV), which evidences that GQDs-PAN nanofibers can weaken the interfacial energy barrier of the Li2S nucleation and growth [55]. The discharge specific capacities of high and low-voltage platforms with the “nucleation point” as the cut-off point are called Q H and Q L, respectively. The specific capacity of the battery mainly comes from the contribution of the Q L, which is closely related to the deposition of Li2S [56]. The ratio of Q L to QH can characterize Li+/e− transmission and LiPSs conversion thoroughly [27]. According to Fig. 3(f), the Q L and Q L/Q H ratio (889.17 mA h g−1, 2.24) of the GQDs-PAN@PP cell are higher than those of the cell based on PP (519.80 mA h g−1, 1.74) and PAN@PP (663.28 mA h g−1, 1.85) separators, which demonstrate that GQDs can effectively chemisorb polysulfide and improve the kinetics of conversion from Li2S x to Li2S/Li2S2 [57]. The cycling performance of the cells with different separators exhibited in Fig. 3(g) was evaluated at 0.1 C. The cell with GQDs-PAN@PP separator delivers a high-capacity retention of 558.09 mA h g−1 after 200 cycles, much higher than the cells with separators based on PP (184.48 mA h g−1) and PAN@PP (318.88 mA h g−1). Furthermore, the cell with GQDs-PAN@PP separator exhibits impressive capacity retention rate (43.27%) and a low average capacity decay rate per cycle (0.28%) (Table S7), indicating the “shuttle effect” was suppressed during the cycles.

Meanwhile, considering practical applications and high energy density for Li-S batteries, the electrochemical performance of cell based on GQDs-PAN@PP separators under high sulfur loading was investigated. The cell still maintained high area capacity of 1.52, 2.24, and 3.23 mA h cm−2 under sulfur loadings of 2.55, 3.82, and 5.10 mg cm−2 after 50 cycles (Fig. 3h), respectively, indicating a high possibility of practical application in Li-S batteries [58]. The cycle stability of the battery was further evaluated at a high current density of 0.5 C for long cycling. As shown in Fig. 3(i), the cells with different separators were repeatedly charged/discharged for more than 500 cycles. The cell with the GQDs-PAN@PP separator delivered an impressive initial discharge capacity of 603.15 mA h g−1 and the final capacity maintained 376.20 mA h g−1 after 500 cycles, with a low decay rate only 0.075% per cycle. In contrast, the cells with PP and PAN@PP separator showed poor initial discharge specific capacity (411.34 mA h g−1, 548.69 mA h g−1) and capacity decay rate (0.179%, 0.148%). The difference can be mainly attributed to the stronger LiPSs adsorbability of GQDs-PAN@PP separator, resulting in enhanced electrochemical properties and elevated sulfur utilization. The outstanding cycling stability and capacity decay of GQDs-PAN@PP separator are better than most modified separators, such as MWCNT/CeO2 (0.14%) [59], PC/MWCNT (0.138%) [60], CuS/G (0.19%) [61], and NiCo2S4@C (0.102%) [62], and summarized in Fig. 3(j) [63–68]. The results verify that GQDs-PAN nanofibers can adsorb LiPSs dissolved in electrolyte and reduce the polarization. In addition, GQDs provides additional active sites to facilitate the continuation of redox reactions, minimizing the loss of active sulfur and improving the cycle stability of the cell.

CV and EIS are important means to characterize electrochemical reaction kinetics, which can directly reflect the reversibility and the charge transfer. The enhancement of electrochemical performance and mechanism of the cell based on GQDs-PAN@PP separator can be further verified. The CV curves of the separators display two cathodic reduction peaks and one anodic oxidation peak, corresponding to the reduction of S8 and the reversible oxidation of Li2S2/Li2S (Fig. 4 a) [22,69]. The cell with GQDs-PAN@PP separators has higher peak current intensity and narrower voltage gap. More importantly, the GQDs-PAN@PP cells had the highest cathodic reduction peak potential and the lowest anodic oxidation peak potential (Fig. 4b). Meanwhile, GQDs-PAN@PP cells expressed a lower Tafel slope (40 mV dec−1 for discharging and 51 mV dec−1 for charging) based on the CV curve evaluated corresponding Tafel slope (Fig. 4c–f). In addition, as the cycle progresses, the CV curves of the GQDs-PAN@PP cells have a higher degree of coincidence than those of the PP and PAN@PP cells, showing higher reversibility and cycling stability, thanks to the synergistic effect of the physical limitation of PAN nanofibers and the chemisorption of GQDs (Fig. 4g and Fig. S16). The results demonstrate that the introduction of GQDs can adsorb LiPSs, reduce the polarization significantly, accelerate the LiPSs transformation kinetics, and alleviate the “shuttle effect” effectively [70,71].

The EIS was conducted to further investigate the charge transfer with the separators (Fig. 4h). The EIS Nyquist plots composed of a semicircle at medium-high frequencies and an inclined line in the low frequencies, corresponding to charge transfer resistance (R ct) and Li+ ions transport, respectively [72]. The equivalent circuit model was constructed and the impedance values were fitted (Fig. S17 and Table S8). The cell based of GQDs-PAN@PP separator revealed the smallest impedance. Meanwhile, the diffusion coefficient of Li+ ion (D Li +) corresponding to EIS was calculated according to the formula (Fig. 4i) [73,74]. It can be observed from Table S8 that the GQDs-PAN@PP cell has a smaller contact resistance (Re , 2.30 Ω) and charge-transfer resistance (R ct, 46.33 Ω) and a larger D Li + (1.95 × 10−11 cm2 s−1) than the cells of the PP and PAN@PP. These results are related to the synergistic effect of the excellent ion transfer ability of GQDs and the abundance of channels and active sites provided by the GQDs-PAN nanofibers network structure. Fast charge transfer and ion conduction effectively reduce “dead sites” and improve reactivity, resulting in excellent electrochemical performance [75]. The consequence testified that GQDs-PAN@PP separators can improve the cycle stability of the batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of sulfiphilic action of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The possible mechanism for the sulfiphilic and lithiophilic of GQDs is shown in Fig. 5 (a). GQDs as a sulfiphilic meditor, the rich functional groups can effectively physically limit and chemically adsorb LiPSs, accelerate ion conduction and reduce the existence of “dead sulfur”. In addition, GQDs as a lithiophilic medium can ensure uniform Li+ flux and surface current field, making GQDs a promising candidate for lithium dendrite inhibitor.

In order to further reveal the sulfiphilic mechanism of GQDs, SEM was conducted on different components (cathode, separator, and anode) of the cell after cycled. As shown in the SEM of cycled cathode based on the GQDs-PAN@PP separators in Fig. 5(d), the particles on the surface were evenly distributed and not encapsulated by LiPSs, exposing more active sites for electrochemical reactions. Whereas, the surface for the cathodes of PP and PAN@PP is coated by LiPSs deposition, which is not conducive to the further progress of electrochemical reaction and is easy to form “dead sulfur”, leading to capacity attenuation (Fig. 5b, c). Meanwhile, the SEM of the separators after cycled can further prove the adsorption of LiPSs by GQDs (Fig. 5e–g). As shown in Fig. 5(g), LiPSs are uniformly adsorbed on the surface of GQDs-PAN fibers, preserving the ion transport channels, which may be due to the effective adsorption of LiPSs by polarity action of GQDs functional groups. Similarly, there is no significant polysulfide deposition on the surface of the separators facing the Li anode side after cycled, and fewer sulfur species can be seen by EDS mapping (Figs. S18 and S19). However, the surface of PP and PAN@PP separators are basically blocked by LiPSs deposition (Fig. 5e and f), which hinders ion transport, resulting in the loss of active substances.

Additionally, the cycled Li anode based with PP and PAN@PP separators shows rough appearance and irregular lump agglomeration on the surface (Fig. 5h and i). The irregular massive may be caused by the LiPSs diffused and deposited on the surface of the Li anode after repeated cycles, resulting in severe corrosion of the anode. In addition, the presence of high-content of sulfur in EDS mapping (Fig. S20a and b), indicating that more sulfur-containing species have unevenly deposited on the surface of Li anode, which hinders ion transfer, increases polarization, and even leads to battery failure [76]. Uniquely, the Li anode of GQDs-PAN@PP cells shown in Fig. 5(j) exhibits a denser and smoother surface film with less corrosion, which can also be confirmed by less amount of sulfur-containing species detected from EDS mapping (Fig. S20c). In conclusion, GQDs effectively block the access of LiPSs to the Li surface and the side reactions between them, inhibit the continuous corrosion, and reduce the irreversible precipitates.

In order to further verify the enhancement of polysulfide adsorption by GQDs-PAN@PP separators, a double-L device was used in a visualization permeation experiment, and blank electrolyte and Li2S6 solution were divided by the separators. For PP (Fig. 6 a) and PAN@PP (Fig. 6b) separators, the Li2S6 solution gradually diffused through the separators into the blank electrolyte, with increasing penetration over time. In contrast, the GQDs-PAN@PP separators penetrated a small amount of Li2S6 after 24 h (Fig. 6c), indicating that the GQDs-PAN@PP separators have strong adsorption and barrier capacity of polysulfides. In order to clarify the sulfiphilicity of GQDs and the LiPSs adsorption mechanism, ex-situ XPS analysis is carried out to reveal the chemical states (Fig. 6d–f). As shown in Fig. 6(e), the C 1s spectra of the cycled GQDs-PAN@PP separators exhibit an obvious peak at 285.35 eV attributed to the CS bond, while the intensity of other functional group peaks (OCO, CO) are relatively low in intensity. The CF3 peak at 291.25 eV originated from the electrolyte salt (lithium bis(trifluoromethane) sulfonimide) [39]. Meanwhile, the S 2p spectra (Fig. 6f) show a CS bond peak at 161.96 eV. The bonding nature indicates that functional groups of GQDs promote the formation of C–S bond and adsorb the LiPSs during the cycling, enabling sulfur to be preserved near the GQDs. It is further proven the sulfiphilic of GQDs.

At the same time, the DFT calculations were carried out with the DMol3 and were used to further reveal the LiPSs adsorption behavior of GQDs and PAN. The optimized molecular models of S8/Li2S x and the matrix (PAN, GQDs, and GQDs-PAN) are shown in Figs. S21 and S22, respectively. The value of binding energy is proportional to the strength of chemical interaction between S8/Li2S x and matrix materials [77]. In Fig. 6(g), the binding energy of GQDs-PAN towards S8 and different Li2S x (Li2S8, Li2S6, Li2S4, Li2S2, and Li2S) are 1.50, 1.68, 1.65, 2.11, 2.47, and 2.68 eV, respectively. The value is higher than the binding energy of GQDs or PAN to S8/Li2S x , meaning that there is strong interaction between GQDs-PAN and S8/Li2S x
[41]. Furthermore, the distribution of electron density between the S8/Li2S x and PAN, GQDs, and GQDs-PAN are shown in Fig. 6(i) and Fig. S23, respectively. It is observed that the electron densities of S8/Li2S x and GQDs-PAN overlap obviously under the isovalue of 0.1, proving a strong chemical interaction between GQDs-PAN and LiPSs. These results further confirmed the strong adsorption effect of GQDs-PAN on LiPSs and revealed the mechanism of improving the electrochemical performance of GQDs-PAN@PP separators and sulfiphilic action of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of lithium affinity of GQDs-PAN@PP separators
ce:section.ce:para: Togetherwith studying the protective effect of GQDs on the lithium anode in Li-S batteries (Fig. 5j), in order to further reveal the lithiophilic mechanism of GQDs, Li//Li symmetrical cell and Li//Cu cells were tested. The effect of GQDs on the dissolution/deposition behavior of Li anode and cycling stability was investigated by Li//Li symmetrical cell test. Fig. 7 (a) displayed the cycling stability of Li//Li symmetric cell with PP, PAN@PP, and GQDs-PAN@PP separators at 0.5 mA cm−2, stripping and plating capacity of 0.5 mA h cm−2. The GQDs-PAN@PP cells generated a low voltage hysteresis of 21 mV with small voltage fluctuation after cycling for 600 h, revealing that GQDs has great potential of ensuring uniform deposition of lithium. However, the cells with PP separator displayed an unstable voltage hysteresis along with unsteady voltage fluctuation and large overpotential (72 mV) after 400 h, indicating the uneven of Li deposition/stripping. As shown in Fig. S24, it is worth noting that the cell of GQDs-PAN@PP separator can cycle stably for a long time at high current densities (1, 2, and 5 mA cm−2). In addition, the Li//Li symmetric cells of GQDs-PAN@PP separators presented a better rate performance than PP and PAN@PP at 0.5, 1, 2, and 4 mA cm−2 (Fig. 7b, c and Fig. S25). Moreover, when the current returned to 0.5 mA cm−2, the cells of GQDs-PAN@PP separators still showed a small overpotential. The stable cycling and rate performance signified that the GQDs can function as an ionic sieve for homogeneous redistribution of Li+ flux and effectively regulate Li deposition [78–80].

Furthermore, ionic conductivity is a crucial parameter for evaluating the physiochemical properties of Li batteries. And the ionic conductivity of separator was obtained by symmetric batteries via EIS (Fig. S26 and Table S9) [81]. The ionic conductivity of GQDs-PAN@PP and PAN@PP separators were 1.09 and 0.70 mS cm−1, respectively, which were much higher than that of PP (0.32 mS cm−1), demonstrating that GQDs/PAN nanofibers increased the Li+ transfer. Simultaneously, the cell of GQDs-PAN@PP separators had a smaller MacMullin number (7.91), indicating a higher electrolyte permeability. The high porosity and fully interconnected network structure formed by the interweaving of nanofibers and the conduction effect of GQDs could improve the absorption of electrolyte and thus the ionic conductivity. As shown in Fig. 7(d) slow Li+ conduction in the cells of PP separator resulted in nonuniform accumulation of Li+ and the formation of protrusion. Subsequently, Li outburst would aggravate the inhomogeneous Li deposition to produce lithium dendrites and eventually lead to short circuit. On the contrary, the cell based on GQDs-PAN@PP separators with faster Li+ migration and uniform deposition induced by GQDs could effectively eliminate the adverse effects and made uniform lithium dissolution/deposition.

To further explore the lithiophilic mechanism of GQDs in-depth, the SEM analysis of Li anode (the side facing away from the GQDs-PAN modification layer) and Cu foil for Li//Li symmetric cells (Fig. 8 a–c) and Li//Cu cells (Fig. 8d–f) after cycling was executed. Notably, in the cells based with GQDs-PAN@PP separators even under a high plating capacity of 1.0 mA h cm−2, a dense, homogeneous, smooth deposition layer with grains of Li bulk is formed on the surface of Li without obvious Li dendrites formation (Fig. 8c). This is closely related to the high Li ions transference number of GQDs-PAN@PP separators up to 0.78 (Fig. 8g, Fig. S27, and Table S10). Visibly, almost all Li deposits formed in PP cells grow into dendrites with different diameters in Fig. 8(a). And these Li dendrites with a looser plating layer, fall short of well-distributed Li deposition. Besides, some of Li deposits in the surface layer are about to detach from the bulk phase, which forms the irreversible Li deposits (“dead Li”) (Figs. S28 and S29). In addition, as shown in Fig. 8(b), needle-like dendrites appear on the Li metal surface of PAN@PP cell, which leads to performance reduced and even short circuit of the battery [80]. These significant variation results extra confirmed the inhibition effect of the GQDs-PAN@PP separators on Li dendrites.

In addition, the cycled Li deposited on Cu substrate in Li//Cu cells was further studied to investigate the impact of GQDs on the deposition morphology in Fig. 8(d–f). The copper foil without lithium deposits has a smooth surface (Fig. S30). And after plating at 1 mA cm−2 with areal capacity of 1.0 mA h cm−2, the morphology of Li deposits shows inherent differences. As shown in Fig. 8(d and e), the deposited Li in the PP and PAN@PP presents a serious heterogeneity including a more porous layer with more Li residues. This is in accordance with the high nucleation overpotential and lower CE (Fig. 8h and i). It is obvious that Li deposits in GQDs-PAN@PP show relatively uniform. Hence, the resultant lithium deposition morphology on the Cu substrate is well correlated with the GQDs [5]. Synthesizing the above theories and analytical result, the possible mechanism of GQDs regulating the homogeneous deposition of Li: (i) the polar functional groups in GQDs guides the homogeneous redeposition of Li and its species; (ii) the synergistic collaboration between GQDs and Li species is forming intact microstructure that partially alleviates the corrosion of Li metals, thereby extending the cycle life.

In summary, we have proposed a synergistic effect separator design strategy for stable and long-life Li-S battery. The multifunctional GQDs-PAN@PP separators solve the critical problem of the sulfur cathode and Li anode. The electrospinning nanofibers and the polar functional groups of GQDs as sulfiphilic mediator effectively inhibited the shuttle of LiPSs and improved the sulfur utilization. Furthermore, GQDs as lithiophilic mediator induced the uniform deposition of Li+ and protected from the damage of the separators caused by the lithium dendrites. The finer interface affinity brought adequate electrochemical active sites and guaranteed rapid electron transfer. The assembled Li-S batteries with GQDs-PAN@PP separators exhibit high sulfur utilization (77%), good reversibility of 558.09 mA h g−1 for 200 cycles, durable cycle life for 500 cycles under 0.5 C (Li//Li symmetric cell stable cycle over 600 h), and excellent cycle stability with ultralow capacity attenuation of 0.075% per cycle. More importantly, impressive areal capacity of 3.23 mA h cm−2 is maintained at high sulfur loading of 5.10 mg cm−2 for 50 cycles. This work provides a convenient and prospective strategy for modified separators, and advances the development of Li-S battery and Li metal battery.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The DFT calculations were supported by Prof. Wei Wang at XAUT and Dr. Jingshuang Dang at SNNU. This work was supported by Key Program (U20A20235) funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52171127, 51974242), Regional Innovation Capability Guidance Program of Shaanxi Provincial Government (2022QFY10-06), Key R&D Program of Xianyang Science and Technology Bureau (2021ZDYF-GY-0029), and Doctoral dissertation innovation fund (310-252072201) by Xi'an University of Technology.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jechem.2023.06.030.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
Supplementary material
